name: Turso API
what_it_does: Turso's platform API and libSQL database protocol for managing edge-deployed SQLite databases with per-tenant database provisioning and global replication.
use_cases: Provisioning per-user or per-tenant SQLite databases at scale
Deploying SQLite databases to the edge closest to users globally
Building multi-tenant SaaS applications with database-per-tenant isolation
Embedded database use cases requiring SQLite compatibility
Serverless applications needing lightweight, fast database access
not_for: Heavy analytical workloads requiring complex joins at scale
Teams needing complex stored procedures or triggers
Very large datasets beyond SQLite's practical limits
best_when: An agent needs to provision or query SQLite-compatible databases at the edge, especially for multi-tenant architectures where each tenant has an isolated database.
avoid_when: You need PostgreSQL features, complex analytics, or very large single-database workloads.

auth.notes: Platform API uses organization-level API tokens. Database access uses database-specific auth tokens. Two-level auth model: platform management vs data plane access.

pricing.notes: Extremely generous free tier for developers. Free plan supports up to 500 separate databases — ideal for multi-tenant testing. No credit card required.

performance.rate_limits: Not strictly documented, depends on plan

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: libSQL protocol errors surface as SQLite errors. Platform API errors are standard JSON. Error messages are generally clear but documentation on edge cases is limited.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: SQLite INSERT OR REPLACE and ON CONFLICT clauses provide full idempotency. Platform operations like database creation are idempotent by name.
known_agent_gotchas: Two-level auth requires managing both platform tokens and database tokens separately
libSQL protocol is SQLite-compatible but not identical — some extensions differ
No webhooks — agents must poll for database changes
Replication lag between primary and replica locations can affect read consistency
Database creation via API requires selecting a region — agents must know target region
SQLite limitations apply: no concurrent writes at high throughput, no partitioning
